findings.title: Missing Token-2022 extension validation before transfer operations
findings.description: The vault_withdraw instruction uses `spl_token_2022::transfer_checked` for a real Token-2022 transfer but performs zero validation of the mint's extension state before executing the transfer. Specifically, it does not verify: (1) whether the mint has a `default_account_state` extension that FROZEN accounts, (2) whether the mint has a `transfer_hook` extension that requires custom CPI routing, or (3) whether the mint has `non_transferable` enabled. Token-2022 mints can have any combination of extensions that fundamentally alter transfer semantics. Calling `transfer_checked` without pre-flight extension validation can result in silent failures, bypassed security checks, or unexpected state changes.
findings.impact: Transfer semantics bypass. If the mint has a transfer_hook extension, the hook's validation logic is bypassed — the transfer proceeds without the hook's checks. If the mint has a frozen default state, accounts may be in an invalid state. If the mint is non-transferable, tokens that should never leave the holder's wallet enter the vault's control. Over time, this enables value extraction from edge cases that should have been blocked.
findings.recommendation: Before any Token-2022 transfer, read the mint's extension data using `StateWithExtensions::<Mint>::unpack(&mint.data.borrow())` and validate: (1) check for `ExtensionType::DefaultAccountState` and verify accounts are not frozen, (2) check for `ExtensionType::TransferHook` and route to the hook's program instead of direct `transfer_checked`, (3) check for `ExtensionType::NonTransferable` and reject vault operations if present. Use the extension validation pattern from the Token-2022 SDK.
